function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Using FY2025 Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA and operating income, calculate the implied EBITDA-to-operating-income gap. Why is satellite depreciation economically recurring even though excluded from Adjusted EBITDA?

steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about SpaceX's FY2025 Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA and operating income, and wants me to:

1. Calculate the implied EBITDA-to-operating-income gap (which would be D&A + other adjustments)
2. Explain why satellite depreciation is economically recurring even though excluded from Adjusted EBITDA

Let me first parse the SpaceX S-1 filing to get the relevant financial data.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Good, the document is loaded. Now let me search for the Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA and operating income figures for FY2025.

arguments.prompt: What is the Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA for FY2025 and the full year 2025? Look for segment-level Adjusted EBITDA figures for the Connectivity segment in {{spacex_s1}}. Report the exact dollar amount.

What is the Connectivity Segment operating income (loss) for FY2025 and the full year 2025? Look for segment-level operating income or operating loss figures for the Connectivity segment in {{spacex_s1}}. Report the exact dollar amount.

results.content:
Based on the provided financial sections, here are the Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA figures:

*   **For the full year 2025 (FY2025):** **$7,168 million**
    *   This is explicitly stated in the preamble: "Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA for 2025 increased by $3,319 million to **$7,168 million** compared to $3,849 million in 2024."
    *   It is also confirmed in the reconciliation table for the "Year Ended December 31, 2025."

*   **For the first quarter of 2025 (Three Months Ended March 31, 2025):** **$1,618 million**
    *   This is provided in both the narrative and the reconciliation table for the "Three Months Ended March 31, 2025."

The fiscal year (FY) 2025 aligns with the calendar year, ending on December 31, 2025. Therefore, the Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA for **FY2025 is $7,168 million**.

## Connectivity Segment Operating Income for FY2025 / Full Year 2025

Based on the MD&A segment data provided:

**Connectivity Income from Operations for the year ended December 31, 2025: $4,423 million**

This is confirmed in multiple places throughout the document:

- The segment-level table shows:
  > **Income (loss) from operations** — Connectivity: **$4,423 million** (for the year ended December 31, 2025)

- The narrative analysis states:
  > "Connectivity income from operations for 2025 **increased by $2,417 million to $4,423 million** compared to $2,006 million for the year ended December 31, 2024"

### Key Context

| Metric | FY2025 | FY2024 | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Connectivity Income from Operations | **$4,423 million** | $2,006 million | +$2,417 million (+120.4%) |

The 120.4% year-over-year growth was driven by:
- **+$2,378 million** in increased consumer subscriber revenue (Starlink)
- **+$1,410 million** in increased enterprise customer revenue
- **Partially offset by** higher depreciation of capitalized launch/satellite costs and higher marketing/international expansion costs

> **Note:** The Connectivity segment operated at a **profit** (income from operations), not a loss, during FY2025. The widened operating loss referenced in the preamble likely pertains to the **AI segment** ($(6,355) million) or the **consolidated** level ($(2,589) million), not the Connectivity segment.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have the key figures:
- Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA for FY2025: $7,168 million
- Connectivity Segment Operating Income for FY2025: $4,423 million

The EBITDA-to-operating-income gap = $7,168M - $4,423M = $2,745 million

This gap represents D&A (depreciation and amortization) and any other adjustments between EBITDA and operating income. Let me also look for the reconciliation table to understand the specific adjustments and find details about satellite depreciation.

arguments.prompt: Retrieve the full Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table for the Connectivity Segment for the year ended December 31, 2025 from {{spacex_s1}}. Show all line items starting from net income or operating income through to Adjusted EBITDA, including depreciation and amortization, stock-based compensation, and any other adjustments. Report exact dollar amounts.

Find information about satellite depreciation, including the useful life of satellites, depreciation methods, and why satellite depreciation may be economically recurring even if excluded from Adjusted EBITDA in {{spacex_s1}}. Look for accounting policy disclosures, satellite constellation capitalization details, and any discussion of satellite replacement capital expenditure.

results.content:
## Connectivity Segment — Adjusted EBITDA Reconciliation Table
### Year Ended December 31, 2025

| (in millions) | Connectivity |
|:---|---:|
| **Income (loss) from operations** | **$4,423** |
| Add: | |
| Depreciation and amortization | 2,376 |
| Share-based compensation | 369 |
| Restructuring charges | — |
| Impairment | — |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$7,168** |

### Verification

$$\$4,423 + \$2,376 + \$369 + \$0 + \$0 = \$7,168$$

### Key Notes

- **Income from operations** of $4,423 million is the starting GAAP measure for the Connectivity segment, derived from revenue of $11,387 million less total costs and expenses of $6,964 million.
- **Depreciation and amortization** of $2,376 million represents the largest add-back, consistent with the capital-intensive nature of the Starlink satellite constellation and ground infrastructure.
- **Share-based compensation** of $369 million was added back as a non-cash charge.
- **Restructuring charges** and **Impairment** were both zero for the Connectivity segment in 2025 (restructuring charges of $487 million were allocated entirely to the AI segment; impairment of $38 million was allocated entirely to the Space segment).
- The resulting **Segment Adjusted EBITDA of $7,168 million** represented a year-over-year increase of **$3,319 million (86.2%)** compared to $3,849 million in 2024, driven primarily by Starlink subscriber growth, increasing enterprise adoption, and improved network efficiency.

Based on a thorough review of the provided document excerpts, here is a comprehensive summary of satellite depreciation accounting, its economic recurrence, and its treatment in the financial statements.

### 1. Depreciation Method and Useful Life for Satellites
*   **Method:** Satellites are depreciated using the **straight-line method**.
*   **Estimated Useful Life:** The Company estimates the useful life of its satellites based on engineering studies, on-orbit performance, propellant life, and planned technology transitions. The specific estimates are:
    *   **Broadband Satellites:** **5 years**
    *   **First-Generation Mobile Satellites:** **3 years**
    *   A general table in the financial notes lists the useful life range for "Satellites" as **3-5 years**.

### 2. Capitalization and Depreciation of Related Costs
*   **Satellite Assets Include:** Costs to build the satellite (parts, labor, overhead) **and capitalized launch costs** incurred by the Space segment to place them into orbit.
*   **Interest Capitalization:** Interest costs are capitalized during the construction period for significant long-term projects (like AI data centers and launch facilities) and become part of the asset's cost, depreciated over its useful life.
*   **Asset Maintenance Policy:** Routine maintenance and repairs are expensed. Only **significant refurbishments or enhancements that extend an asset's life or increase its functionality** are capitalized and depreciated over the revised useful life.

### 3. Why Satellite Depreciation is Economically Recurring (Even When Excluded from EBITDA)
Satellite depreciation is excluded from **Adjusted EBITDA** (a non-GAAP measure) as it is a non-cash expense. However, it represents a **fundamental and recurring economic cost** of the business for two key reasons:

*   **Asset Replenishment Cycle:** The documents explicitly state that the useful life of satellites is **inherently shorter** than that of the terrestrial IT systems they host. Consequently, the Company **must periodically launch replacement satellites** as existing ones reach the end of their lives. This creates a continuous cycle of capital expenditure that is economically mandatory to maintain service capacity.
*   **Link to Capital Expenditure:** Depreciation is an accounting allocation of past capital spending (CapEx). The recurring need to replace satellites ensures that **significant future capital expenditures will be incurred**. For example, the Capital Expenditure table shows large, ongoing investments in the Connectivity segment (which includes Starlink).
*   **Risk Disclosure:** The risk factors note that events like space weather can cause "accelerated depreciation, decommissioning or need for replacement," directly linking the risk of asset impairment to the need for future capital outlays.

### 4. Financial Statement Impact and EBITDA Treatment
*   **Expense Recognition:** Depreciation is recorded in the **Consolidated Statements of Operations**. For instance, total depreciation expense was $2,329 million for the three months ended March 31, 2026.
*   **Segment Reporting:** Depreciation is a major component of the **Connectivity segment's operating expenses**. For example, the Connectivity segment's depreciation was $783 million for Q1 2026.
*   **EBITDA Reconciliation:** In both consolidated and segment reconciliations, **depreciation and amortization are added back** to net income or segment operating income to calculate Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA. This is explicitly shown in the reconciliation tables.
    *   **Rationale:** This exclusion allows investors to view the Company's operational profitability and cash-generating ability without the influence of non-cash accounting charges for past capital investments. The recurring nature of these costs is instead reflected in the **Capital Expenditure** line items on the cash flow statement and balance sheet.

### Summary
In essence, while satellite depreciation is a **non-cash accounting expense** excluded from EBITDA metrics, it is a **systemic indicator of the Company's recurring economic reality**. It signals the continuous consumption and required replacement of a core productive asset. The accounting policy of straight-line depreciation over 3-5 years systematically allocates the cost of these assets, while the underlying business model ensures that future capital outlays will be necessary to replace depreciating satellites and maintain operational capacity.

steps.reasoning_content: Now I have all the information I need. Let me also retrieve additional details about capital expenditure for the Connectivity segment to reinforce the economic recurrence argument.

arguments.prompt: Retrieve the capital expenditure figures for the Connectivity Segment for FY2024 and FY2025 from {{spacex_s1}}. Also find any disclosure about satellite replacement CapEx or maintenance CapEx versus growth CapEx in the Connectivity segment.

results.content:
Based on the provided MD&A sections and financial tables, here are the requested figures:

### Capital Expenditures for the Connectivity Segment
*   **For FY2024:** **$3,498 million**.
*   **For FY2025:** **$4,178 million**.

*(Sources: Capital expenditure tables found in the Preamble and Notes to Financial Statements.)*

### Disclosure on Satellite Replacement vs. Growth CapEx
The provided MD&A narrative does **not** contain a specific breakdown between "replacement CapEx" and "maintenance CapEx" versus "growth CapEx" for the Connectivity segment.

However, the explanation for the year-over-year increases in Connectivity capital expenditures consistently states the primary driver was investment in expansion and growth. The relevant disclosure is:

> "The increase in each year-over-year period was primarily driven by higher satellite and ground equipment costs as we continue to increase our number of satellites and grow our satellite network."

This indicates the disclosed capital expenditures are predominantly for **growth** (expanding the satellite network and ground systems) rather than for sustaining or replacing existing assets. The disclosure focuses on the total capital investment and its growth-oriented purpose, without a detailed separation of maintenance from expansion costs.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the information needed to provide a comprehensive answer. Let me calculate the EBITDA-to-operating-income gap and explain the economic recurrence of satellite depreciation.

Key figures:
- Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA (FY2025): $7,168 million
- Connectivity Segment Operating Income (FY2025): $4,423 million
- Gap: $7,168M - $4,423M = $2,745 million

Reconciliation:
- D&A: $2,376 million
- SBC: $369 million
- Total add-backs: $2,745 million

Let me calculate this precisely.

Perfect. The gap is $2,745 million, which equals $2,376M (D&A) + $369M (SBC) = $2,745M. This confirms the reconciliation.

Now let me also calculate D&A as a percentage of the gap and operating income.

So D&A represents about 86.6% of the EBITDA-to-operating-income gap.

Let me also calculate the ratio of D&A to operating income and to revenue.
Now I have all the information needed. Let me prepare a comprehensive answer.

arguments.final_result:
## FY2025 Connectivity Segment: EBITDA-to-Operating-Income Gap Analysis

### Key Figures

| Metric | FY2025 ($M) |
|:---|---:|
| Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA | $7,168 |
| Connectivity Segment Income from Operations | $4,423 |
| **EBITDA-to-Operating-Income Gap** | **$2,745** |

### Reconciliation Bridge

The $2,745 million gap is composed entirely of add-backs in the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation:

| Add-back Component | Amount ($M) | % of Gap |
|:---|---:|---:|
| Depreciation & Amortization | $2,376 | 86.6% |
| Share-Based Compensation | $369 | 13.4% |
| Restructuring Charges | $0 | 0.0% |
| Impairment | $0 | 0.0% |
| **Total Add-backs** | **$2,745** | **100.0%** |

**Verification:** $4,423 + $2,376 + $369 = $7,168 ✓

### Interpretation of the Gap

The $2,376 million D&A add-back dominates the gap, representing **86.6%** of the total difference and equating to **53.7% of Connectivity operating income**. This reflects the capital-intensive nature of operating a global satellite constellation (Starlink) with significant depreciating assets.

The $369 million share-based compensation add-back is comparatively modest (13.4% of the gap), indicating that stock-based pay is not a major driver of the segment's non-GAAP adjustments.

---

### Why Satellite Depreciation is Economically Recurring

Even though satellite depreciation is excluded from Adjusted EBITDA as a "non-cash" charge, it represents a **genuine, recurring economic cost** for several interconnected reasons:

**1. Short Useful Life Creates Mandatory Replacement Cycles**

The S-1 discloses that SpaceX satellites have estimated useful lives of only **3–5 years** (3 years for first-generation mobile satellites; 5 years for broadband satellites). This is far shorter than terrestrial infrastructure assets. The Company states that satellite useful life is "inherently shorter than that of the terrestrial IT systems they host." This means the fleet requires **continuous replacement** just to maintain current service capacity—there is no option to defer satellite CapEx without degrading the network.

**2. Depreciation Reflects Real Capital Consumption**

Satellite depreciation (straight-line over 3–5 years) systematically allocates the cost of building and launching each satellite. While it is an accounting non-cash expense in the period it is recorded, it represents the **actual consumption of a finite productive asset**. The satellite physically degrades (propellant depletion, component wear, orbital decay), and the depreciation schedule mirrors this real economic decline.

**3. Recurring CapEx Necessity**

Because satellites have short lives and must be replaced, the depreciation recognized in each period is a **leading indicator of future mandatory capital expenditure**. Even though SpaceX does not separately disclose "replacement CapEx" versus "growth CapEx," the physics of the problem are unambiguous: once a satellite reaches end-of-life, it must be replaced or service capacity declines. Connectivity segment CapEx was $4,178 million in FY2025—substantially exceeding D&A of $2,376 million—suggesting the Company is both replacing depreciated assets *and* growing the constellation.

**4. EBITDA Masks a Structural Cash Outflow**

EBITDA's exclusion of D&A is justified on the grounds that it is a non-cash charge. But when the underlying assets have 3–5 year lives and require continuous replenishment, the "non-cash" characterization is misleading at the segment level. The Company will spend billions annually on satellite CapEx in perpetuity just to stand still. Ignoring this by looking only at EBITDA overstates the segment's true economic profitability and cash generation capacity.

**5. Risk Disclosure Confirms Economic Recurrence**

The S-1 risk factors explicitly note that space weather and other hazards can cause "accelerated depreciation, decommissioning or need for replacement"—directly linking satellite depreciation to the economic reality of required future capital outlays.

### Analytical Conclusion

The $2,745 million EBITDA-to-operating-income gap, driven predominantly by $2,376 million in D&A, is not a mere accounting artifact. It reflects the **fundamental economics of operating a short-lived satellite constellation** where assets must be continuously replaced. Investors should treat Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA ($7,168 million) with caution and consider that a substantial portion of the D&A add-back represents economically recurring costs that will manifest as ongoing capital expenditure, not as true "excess" cash flow.

---

**Sources:**
- SpaceX S-1 Filing (May 20, 2026): https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm
  - Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table (Connectivity, FY2025)
  - Segment operating income disclosure (Connectivity, FY2025)
  - Satellite useful life and depreciation policy (Notes to Financial Statements)
  - Capital expenditure tables (Connectivity segment, FY2024–FY2025)
  - Risk factors regarding satellite replacement and space hazards

results.content:
## FY2025 Connectivity Segment: EBITDA-to-Operating-Income Gap Analysis

### Key Figures

| Metric | FY2025 ($M) |
|:---|---:|
| Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA | $7,168 |
| Connectivity Segment Income from Operations | $4,423 |
| **EBITDA-to-Operating-Income Gap** | **$2,745** |

### Reconciliation Bridge

The $2,745 million gap is composed entirely of add-backs in the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation:

| Add-back Component | Amount ($M) | % of Gap |
|:---|---:|---:|
| Depreciation & Amortization | $2,376 | 86.6% |
| Share-Based Compensation | $369 | 13.4% |
| Restructuring Charges | $0 | 0.0% |
| Impairment | $0 | 0.0% |
| **Total Add-backs** | **$2,745** | **100.0%** |

**Verification:** $4,423 + $2,376 + $369 = $7,168 ✓

### Interpretation of the Gap

The $2,376 million D&A add-back dominates the gap, representing **86.6%** of the total difference and equating to **53.7% of Connectivity operating income**. This reflects the capital-intensive nature of operating a global satellite constellation (Starlink) with significant depreciating assets.

The $369 million share-based compensation add-back is comparatively modest (13.4% of the gap), indicating that stock-based pay is not a major driver of the segment's non-GAAP adjustments.

---

### Why Satellite Depreciation is Economically Recurring

Even though satellite depreciation is excluded from Adjusted EBITDA as a "non-cash" charge, it represents a **genuine, recurring economic cost** for several interconnected reasons:

**1. Short Useful Life Creates Mandatory Replacement Cycles**

The S-1 discloses that SpaceX satellites have estimated useful lives of only **3–5 years** (3 years for first-generation mobile satellites; 5 years for broadband satellites). This is far shorter than terrestrial infrastructure assets. The Company states that satellite useful life is "inherently shorter than that of the terrestrial IT systems they host." This means the fleet requires **continuous replacement** just to maintain current service capacity—there is no option to defer satellite CapEx without degrading the network.

**2. Depreciation Reflects Real Capital Consumption**

Satellite depreciation (straight-line over 3–5 years) systematically allocates the cost of building and launching each satellite. While it is an accounting non-cash expense in the period it is recorded, it represents the **actual consumption of a finite productive asset**. The satellite physically degrades (propellant depletion, component wear, orbital decay), and the depreciation schedule mirrors this real economic decline.

**3. Recurring CapEx Necessity**

Because satellites have short lives and must be replaced, the depreciation recognized in each period is a **leading indicator of future mandatory capital expenditure**. Even though SpaceX does not separately disclose "replacement CapEx" versus "growth CapEx," the physics of the problem are unambiguous: once a satellite reaches end-of-life, it must be replaced or service capacity declines. Connectivity segment CapEx was $4,178 million in FY2025—substantially exceeding D&A of $2,376 million—suggesting the Company is both replacing depreciated assets *and* growing the constellation.

**4. EBITDA Masks a Structural Cash Outflow**

EBITDA's exclusion of D&A is justified on the grounds that it is a non-cash charge. But when the underlying assets have 3–5 year lives and require continuous replenishment, the "non-cash" characterization is misleading at the segment level. The Company will spend billions annually on satellite CapEx in perpetuity just to stand still. Ignoring this by looking only at EBITDA overstates the segment's true economic profitability and cash generation capacity.

**5. Risk Disclosure Confirms Economic Recurrence**

The S-1 risk factors explicitly note that space weather and other hazards can cause "accelerated depreciation, decommissioning or need for replacement"—directly linking satellite depreciation to the economic reality of required future capital outlays.

### Analytical Conclusion

The $2,745 million EBITDA-to-operating-income gap, driven predominantly by $2,376 million in D&A, is not a mere accounting artifact. It reflects the **fundamental economics of operating a short-lived satellite constellation** where assets must be continuously replaced. Investors should treat Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA ($7,168 million) with caution and consider that a substantial portion of the D&A add-back represents economically recurring costs that will manifest as ongoing capital expenditure, not as true "excess" cash flow.
